content:
Formal treatment of **missing data** accelerated in the late 20th century as survey research and biomedical studies confronted non-response. **Donald Rubin** (1976) formalized **MCAR**, **MAR**, and **MNAR** and showed when complete-case analysis is unbiased versus when **imputation** or **likelihood-based** methods are needed.

**Multiple imputation** (Rubin 1987) propagated uncertainty by generating several filled datasets instead of a single deterministic guess. **EM algorithm** and **MICE** (Multivariate Imputation by Chained Equations) became standard tools for MAR settings in statistics packages.

Machine learning pipelines adopted pragmatic shortcuts—**mean/median fill**, **KNN impute**, **forward-fill** on time series—often inside reusable **sklearn** transformers. The same **train-only fit** discipline used for scaling was applied to imputers to prevent **leakage**.

Modern **gradient-boosted trees** added **learned default directions** for NaN at split time, giving practitioners a third path besides deletion and classical imputation when missingness is frequent and structured.

timeline.label: Rubin's framework
timeline.description: MCAR/MAR/MNAR classification clarifies when deletion is safe and when imputation must account for mechanism.

timeline.label: Multiple imputation
timeline.description: MICE and related methods spread uncertainty across imputed draws instead of a single filled table.

timeline.label: ML pipelines
timeline.description: SimpleImputer and Pipeline standardize train-only statistics; tree libraries learn NaN split behavior natively.
